title: Affordability: Suncorp chief slams NSW tax hit on home premiums
full_text:
The chief executive of Suncorp's consumer insurance business says the NSW government's policy of hiding an emergency service tax inside premiums is unfair to customers struggling to afford the rising cost of cover.
Lisa Harrison, whose business includes brands such as AAMI and GIO, said nearly half of a $1000 premium goes to taxes in NSW, a major issue as more residents find it difficult to buy insurance as inflation pushes up costs.
The emergency service levy - about 18 per cent of premiums - is being reviewed by the Minns government amid concerns that it unfairly places the burden of paying for essential services only on those with insurance.
"We all know it's a big tax. If you pay $1000 on your insurance, your risk premium with GST, stamp duty and the emergency services levy, [you're] potentially paying another $400 that's collected as part of the insurance premium. That's a lot of money," Harrison told The Australian Financial Review Insurance Summit in Sydney on Tuesday.
"In NSW, there's a lot of tax on insurance, and it's been over 950 days since the Minns government announced reform to the emergency service levy ... and we still don't have that legislation passed that would make a meaningful difference on home insurance policies and small business policies.
"It's an unfair tax if you're in Lismore ... You're paying more than the person who's insured their home for a million dollars in Balmain."
The cost of the NSW levy for residential insurance has increased by 48 per cent between 2018 and 2024. It is the only state apart from Tasmania that has not switched to property-based levies to fund emergency services.
Labor's preferred method of paying for emergency services is through a levy on land values, forcing property owners to pay up to $627 per year.
NSW Building Minister Anoulack Chanthivong told the summit the government was aware of the inequity with the current levy scheme.
"There is potential room for reform and hopefully the government, with the Treasury, will make some announcements on that shortly," Chanthivong said.
Harrison said Australia had a risk management problem with homes built in the wrong places.
"We're dealing with, unfortunately, homes built in wrong places, not built to the right standards and we haven't invested enough in mitigation ... we do see pockets of areas where insurance affordability is a challenge."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucy Slade
heading: General Insurance
